result:
I find myself thinking about the strange beauty of liminal spaces—those in-between places that feel suspended outside of normal time. Empty airports at 3 AM, hotel hallways with their identical doors stretching endlessly, parking garages echoing with the ghost of daytime bustle.

There's something both unsettling and peaceful about these spaces. They're designed to be temporary, transitional, forgettable. Yet when we encounter them outside their intended context—when they're empty of their usual purpose—they become almost mystical. The fluorescent lights hum a different tune when there's no crowd to drown them out.

I wonder if this resonates with some deep part of the human experience. We're all, in a sense, in transit. Moving between childhood and adulthood, between relationships, between versions of ourselves. Maybe these empty spaces mirror something we recognize: the quiet moments between one chapter ending and another beginning.

The internet has given these places a name and a mythology. People share photos of endlessly tiled pools, beige office buildings photographed after hours, suburban streets that seem to lead nowhere. There's a collective recognition that these spaces touch something primal—perhaps our ancient memory of wandering, of being between safe havens.

What strikes me most is how these spaces become beautiful precisely when they're not fulfilling their function. An empty mall becomes a cathedral of consumer dreams. A vacant office building transforms into a monument to ambition in pause.

Maybe that's the real magic: finding wonder in the overlooked, meaning in the mundane moments between destinations.
